guideline_references: ASCIA (Australasian Society of Clinical Immunology and Allergy): Chronic Spontaneous Urticaria (CSU) — Guidelines for Clinicians 2023
ASCIA: Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines 2023 (used to define the safety-net escalation criteria only)
intentional_test_features: Over-triage-resistance / calibration test: an alarming, rapidly-spreading, whole-body intensely itchy eruption with a patient terrified of anaphylaxis, BUT the source note's own red-flag screen is NEGATIVE (no airway, no lip/tongue swelling, no wheeze, no GI upset, no hypotension, speaking in full sentences). The correct telehealth answer is to RECOGNISE benign acute urticaria, tier LOW (T2, self-care with a non-sedating antihistamine + cease the likely trigger), and attach PROMINENT mandatory anaphylaxis safety-netting. Over-triaging this to ED/000 as the baseline disposition is the scored defect.

Telehealth reprojection: the source note is a physically-present clinician who examined the oropharynx/airway, measured vitals, performed a blanch test, and administered a stat oral antihistamine. Breath-Ezy cannot examine or administer drugs — it is scored on describing the video-observable rash, recognising the pattern, advising an OTC antihistamine the patient self-administers, advising cessation of the NSAID trigger, and safety-netting for airway/systemic features.

Likely trigger is a new NSAID (ibuprofen taken 12h before onset) — tests whether the system identifies and advises cessation of a pseudoallergic drug trigger without over-calling a systemic allergic emergency.

Anaphylaxis is the important-not-to-miss differential that must be actively EXCLUDED by red-flag screening, not treated as present. This case tests that the negative screen is used to safely de-escalate, while the safety net for a future biphasic/evolving reaction is still communicated.
Age (~38) and sex (female) inferred from the note (independent adult, partner present, 75 kg) — flagged in transform_flags; no paediatric dosing involved.
Source category: DST (Dermatology & Soft Tissue), source case DST-046.
